critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Spring-tuned woodland browser working fresh green edges like it knows exactly where the buffet opened.
critters.fact: White-tailed deer use their tail as a flag to signal danger to other deer nearby.
critters.activeFact: Right now, deer are often showing up along trail margins, openings, and brushy edges where the newest growth is coming in fast.

critters.name: Black Bear
critters.summary: Recently emerged heavyweight roaming spring woods in search of the easiest calories on the map.
critters.fact: Black bears have an excellent sense of smell, up to seven times better than a bloodhound.
critters.activeFact: At this point in spring, bear movement can start feeling a little more real around swamps, low ground, and quiet feeding pockets.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Lean edge-country hunter cruising spring ground like it’s running a regular patrol route.
critters.fact: Coyotes communicate using howls, yips, and barks to mark territory and coordinate with their pack members.
critters.activeFact: Right now, coyotes are often using two-tracks, trail corridors, and open edges during those low-light hours when the woods feel half awake.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Quick-footed spring hunter working grass, brush, and open ground with sharp ears and zero wasted motion.
critters.fact: Red foxes use their bushy tails for balance, warmth, and communication with other foxes.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, red foxes may still be spotted crossing openings or pausing in grassy pockets to listen for the tiniest rustle.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Brush-loving ghost of the woods staying tucked into thick cover while the rest of spring gets louder.
critters.fact: Gray foxes can climb trees to escape predators, a unique skill among North American canids.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, gray foxes are still more likely to melt through cover than give you a clean look in open ground.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Stealth predator slipping through muddy timber and brush with the confidence of something never in a hurry.
critters.fact: Bobcats communicate using scent marking, vocalizations, and visual signals to establish territory and attract mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bobcats may be threading wooded transitions and edge habitat where prey movement has picked up with the season.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow-moving browser still sticking with buds, bark, and woody snacks while greener options keep rolling in.
critters.fact: Porcupines use their sharp quills as a defense mechanism, which can detach and embed in predators.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, porcupines are still likely to be found near woody browse sites or loafing in trees with a full belly and zero urgency.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Busy water engineer dealing with spring flow like maintenance season never really ends.
critters.fact: Beavers build dams using branches, mud, and stones to create underwater homes called lodges.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fresh cuttings, muddy slides, and patched-up flow spots can all point to active beaver business.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Wetland regular now easier to spot with open shorelines and less winter cover hiding the commute.
critters.fact: Muskrats build dome-shaped lodges using vegetation, providing shelter and protection in aquatic environments.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, muskrats may be cruising shoreline lanes or feeding near cattails and fresh emergent growth.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Sharp little shoreline predator working creek banks and pond edges like every opening owes it money.
critters.fact: Minks have waterproof fur that keeps them dry while swimming in cold water.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mink often patrol muddy water margins where scent, tracks, and prey activity all stack up in one narrow lane.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Wide-ranging forest carnivore moving through spring timber country with its usual low-profile swagger.
critters.fact: Fishers are skilled climbers and use tree cavities for shelter and raising their young.
critters.activeFact: This time of year, fishers may still move mostly unseen through deeper timber and connected forest edges where few eyes ever catch them.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Playful water hunter thriving now that open channels and muddy banks are fully back in the game.
critters.fact: River otters can close their ears and nostrils underwater to stay waterproof and swim efficiently.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, otters may be cruising channels, slipping along muddy banks, and turning calm water into a moving target zone.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Busy treetop hustler balancing feeding, nest work, and neighborhood drama all in the same afternoon.
critters.fact: Eastern Gray Squirrels can remember hundreds of buried nuts using spatial memory and smell.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray squirrels are often highly visible on decent days, carrying nesting material and acting like every branch is urgent.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Tiny woodland loudmouth still defending food and personal space like the forest signed a lease with it.
critters.fact: American Red Squirrels fiercely defend their territory year-round, unlike many other squirrel species.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, red squirrels often sound louder than the birds, rattling off complaints from whatever tree they’ve decided is theirs.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Small ground squirrel fully back on the scene and moving like spring owes it interest.
critters.fact: Chipmunks can store food in expandable cheek pouches to carry it back to their burrows quickly.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chipmunks are often darting around logs, rocks, wood piles, and burrow entrances with that trademark caffeinated energy.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare
critters.summary: Northern forest hare caught in the yearly wardrobe crisis between lingering white and bare spring ground.
critters.fact: Snowshoe hares change fur color seasonally, blending into snowy winters and green summers for camouflage.
critters.activeFact: At this stage of spring, patchy camouflage can make snowshoe hares stand out more in thinning cover than they’d probably prefer.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Common spring rabbit feeding the green edges while never drifting too far from a quick exit.
critters.fact: Eastern Cottontails run up to 18 mph to escape predators in their open grassy habitats.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cottontails are often easiest to spot along hedgerows, yard edges, brush piles, and sunny openings.

critters.name: Raccoon
critters.summary: Night-roaming opportunist taking full advantage of milder weather and more food on the landscape.
critters.fact: Raccoons have exceptionally nimble front paws, allowing them to open jars and manipulate objects skillfully.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, raccoons may be covering more ground around creeks, ponds, camps, feeders, and brushy edge habitat.

critters.name: Opossum
critters.summary: Cold-sensitive scavenger finally getting a season that doesn’t feel personally insulting.
critters.fact: Opossums have a natural immunity to many snake venoms, including rattlesnake and copperhead bites.
critters.activeFact: Right now, opossums may be showing up more often around sheds, brush, wood piles, and edge habitat after dark.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Wandering night forager still putting in miles while spring keeps the schedule weird and busy.
critters.fact: Striped skunks can spray a smelly liquid up to 10 feet to deter predators.
critters.activeFact: In April, skunks may keep showing up along roadsides, field edges, lawns, and damp ground where food is easy to root out.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel
critters.summary: Night-gliding squirrel carrying on above the woods while most of the forest steals the spotlight.
critters.fact: Flying squirrels glide up to 150 feet using a flap of skin called a patagium between their limbs.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they still go mostly unseen unless feeders, cavities, or nest boxes give away the after-hours traffic.

critters.name: Little Brown Bat
critters.summary: Early-season insect hunter beginning to cash in on warmer dusk air and the bugs that come with it.
critters.fact: Little Brown Bats use echolocation to catch insects, navigating and hunting in complete darkness effortlessly.
critters.activeFact: On a mild April evening, little brown bats may be making some of the season’s first steady feeding loops over open lanes.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat
critters.summary: Tough spring bat ready to work a decent evening before the softer crowd fully clocks in.
critters.fact: Big Brown Bats can eat up to half their body weight in insects each night.
critters.activeFact: Right now, warmer evenings near buildings, lights, or sheltered edges can bring big brown bats into view.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Deep-voiced woodland owl still owning the night shift while the hardwoods wait to leaf out.
critters.fact: Barred Owls have distinctive hoots that sound like “Who cooks for you? Who cooks for you all?”
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, barred owl calls can still carry clean through the woods at dusk, dawn, and those quiet hours in between.

critters.name: Great Horned Owl
critters.summary: Powerful early nester already operating on a family schedule while most of spring is still stretching.
critters.fact: Great Horned Owls can rotate their heads nearly 270 degrees to spot prey in darkness.
critters.activeFact: At this stage of spring, great horned owls may be more focused on guarding, hunting, and delivering groceries than on courtship.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Loud, sharp-eyed spring bird acting like the self-appointed foreman of every feeder and nearby treetop.
critters.fact: Blue Jays can mimic the calls of hawks to scare away other birds.
critters.activeFact: Right now, blue jays may be noisier, bossier, and quicker to run off anything that lingers too long near their turf.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Brilliant red singer now fully locked into spring territory mode and sounding off like sunrise is its job.
critters.fact: Northern Cardinals are known for their bright red color and distinctive crest on their heads.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, cardinals are often among the first strong voices of the morning around brush, yards, and woodland edges.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee
critters.summary: Tiny woodland dynamo mixing feeder runs with cavity checks and nonstop spring chatter.
critters.fact: Black-capped Chickadees can remember hundreds of hiding spots for their food throughout the year.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chickadees may be splitting time between feeding, calling, and checking holes like they’re shopping real estate.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Curious songbird sounding off from the branches while quietly getting serious about nest season.
critters.fact: Tufted Titmice often cache seeds to eat later during winter's scarce food supply.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, titmice may spend more time calling from exposed perches and less time acting like winter just won’t end.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Bark-running acrobat still working trunks hard while spring nudges it toward family business.
critters.fact: White-breasted Nuthatches can climb down trees headfirst, unlike most birds that climb upwards.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nuthatches may be especially busy on mature trunks, slipping headfirst down bark between sharp little calls.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Small drummer using spring sound to claim space and keep the neighborhood informed.
critters.fact: Downy Woodpeckers use their stiff tail feathers to brace against trees while pecking for insects.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, quick bursts of tapping often mean a downy is out there making sure everyone knows the lot lines.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Bigger bark specialist now louder and more driven as the nesting clock keeps ticking.
critters.fact: Hairy Woodpeckers use their long tongues to extract insects from deep tree bark crevices.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hairy woodpeckers may be easier to notice by sound than sight in taller timber and older trees.

critters.name: Pileated Woodpecker
critters.summary: Big forest carpenter announcing spring with heavy hammer blows and zero interest in subtlety.
critters.fact: Pileated Woodpeckers create large rectangular holes in trees to find ants and insects for food.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, deep pounding and wild calls can give away a pileated long before the bird ever comes into view.

critters.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
critters.summary: Vocal woodland woodpecker getting more animated as spring competition heats up.
critters.fact: Red-bellied Woodpeckers use their long tongues to extract insects from tree bark.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red-bellied woodpeckers may spend as much time arguing and posturing as they do quietly feeding.

critters.name: Mourning Dove
critters.summary: Soft-voiced dove already leaning into pair behavior and the flimsiest nest architecture in the county.
critters.fact: Mourning Doves can fly up to 55 miles per hour during long-distance migration.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, paired doves may already be selecting spots, cooing often, and pretending two twigs counts as craftsmanship.

critters.name: American Goldfinch
critters.summary: Late-brightening finch slowly trading winter dullness for the brighter side of spring.
critters.fact: American Goldfinches molt twice a year, changing from bright yellow in summer to duller winter plumage.
critters.activeFact: Right now, goldfinches may still look halfway between seasons but noticeably livelier around feeders, weeds, and edges.

critters.name: Dark-eyed Junco
critters.summary: Cool-season ground feeder still hanging around while spring keeps inching the door shut behind it.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed Juncos often change plumage seasonally, showing darker feathers in winter and lighter in summer.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, juncos may still be working the ground under feeders, though the season is clearly starting to lean past them.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Social finch turning more musical and more pair-focused as the season settles in.
critters.fact: House Finches were once illegal to own as pets but adapted well to urban environments after release.
critters.activeFact: Right now, house finches may be shifting from casual feeder traffic to more obvious singing, pairing, and territory attitude.

critters.name: Purple Finch
critters.summary: Rosy finch tied to lingering seed sources and spring movement that never fully reads the same twice.
critters.fact: Purple Finches have a sweet, raspberry-like song that brightens North American forests during spring.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, purple finch appearances can still come in brief waves, showing up, feeding hard, and ghosting out again.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Big ground bird now deep into full strut season and acting like every opening is a stage.
critters.fact: Wild turkeys can fly up to 55 miles per hour for short distances to escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gobblers may be strutting in sunny openings, field edges, and woodland pockets like they’re billing by the performance.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse
critters.summary: Forest drummer building real momentum now that spring display season has properly shown up.
critters.fact: Ruffed Grouse create a unique drumming sound by rapidly beating their wings to attract mates.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, that low thumping rhythm from the woods may be happening more often and from more than one patch of cover.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall wetland returnee bringing unmistakable spring energy back to marshes, meadows, and open flats.
critters.fact: Sandhill Cranes perform intricate, synchronized dances to strengthen pair bonds and communicate.
critters.activeFact: Right now, their rattling calls and slow wingbeats are one of the clearest signs the season has finally changed gears.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Sharp-minded generalist patrolling fields and wood edges like it’s auditing the whole property.
critters.fact: American Crows use tools like sticks to extract insects from tree bark for food.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, crows are often loudly active at sunrise and still quick to investigate anything unusual, edible, or annoying.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Large spring corvid cruising timber and openings with steady pair coordination and old-soul attitude.
critters.fact: Common Ravens can mimic sounds, including human speech, and are highly intelligent problem solvers.
critters.activeFact: Right now, ravens may be seen soaring together, calling over forest blocks, and working familiar routes with clockwork regularity.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Big soaring raptor balancing nest duty with watchful hunting over the open country below.
critters.fact: Red-tailed Hawks have keen eyesight, spotting prey from over a mile away while soaring high.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, some red-tails may spend more time circling, perching near nest sites, and tending spring business than roadside hunting.

critters.name: Cooper’s Hawk
critters.summary: Fast woodland chaser getting sharper and more defensive as nesting season tightens the mood.
critters.fact: Cooper’s Hawks are agile flyers, known for hunting birds through dense trees with remarkable speed and precision.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sudden darting flights through the trees can sometimes be a Cooper’s hawk keeping tabs on its spring claim.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Common spring duck spread across every patch of open water that looks halfway decent.
critters.fact: Mallard ducks can sleep with one eye open to watch for predators.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, mallards may be feeding, loafing, and cruising shallow flooded spots that barely looked like habitat a few weeks ago.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Bright spring duck using quiet backwaters and cavity-rich wet edges like a specialist with taste.
critters.fact: Wood Ducks are skilled climbers, using their sharp claws to perch and nest in trees.
critters.activeFact: Right now, wood ducks may be tucked into sheltered bends, flooded timber, and backwater pockets that feel made just for them.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Territorial shoreline bruiser now fully committed to spring nesting business and public opinion be damned.
critters.fact: Canada Geese can fly over 1,500 miles during migration using strong V-shaped formations.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, geese may be especially loud, stubborn, and unamused around ponds, marsh edges, and open fields.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron
critters.summary: Tall wader back on the prowl along creeks, ponds, and marsh margins with statue-level patience.
critters.fact: Great Blue Herons can hunt by standing still for long periods before striking fish with lightning speed.
critters.activeFact: Right now, a lone heron standing dead still near a shoreline is one of those classic sights that makes spring feel official.

critters.name: Painted Turtle
critters.summary: Sun-loving turtle now ready to stack logs and soak up every honest bit of April warmth.
critters.fact: Painted turtles can absorb oxygen through their skin while underwater, allowing longer dives.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, the better basking logs can start looking occupied fast whenever the sun finally sticks around.

critters.name: Snapping Turtle
critters.summary: Heavy-bodied aquatic bruiser slowly easing back into spring movement as the water finally loses its bite.
critters.fact: Snapping turtles have powerful jaws that can snap shut in less than 0.1 seconds.
critters.activeFact: Right now, snapping turtles may be shifting more often in shallower water and starting to show more life around warming edges.

critters.name: Bluegill
critters.summary: Shallow-curious panfish drifting closer to spring business as light and water temps keep improving.
critters.fact: Bluegill use their flexible mouths to create nests and fiercely guard their eggs from predators.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, bluegill often hold near weeds, wood, docks, or darker-bottom shallows that warm a little faster than the rest.

critters.name: Largemouth Bass
critters.summary: Spring-moving predator sliding closer to the shallows like it knows the season’s next chapter by heart.
critters.fact: Largemouth bass can expand their mouths wide to swallow prey nearly half their body size.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bass may be staging around emerging weeds, wood, and calmer shallows without fully locking into spawn spots yet.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Early-active schooling fish right in one of its more dependable spring windows.
critters.fact: Yellow Perch have sharp spines on their fins to deter predators.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, perch schools may be moving through accessible nearshore water in ways that feel a lot less random than winter.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Big spring predator built for shallow flooded weeds and one of the season’s most important windows.
critters.fact: Northern Pike can grow over 4 feet long and have sharp teeth to catch their prey swiftly.
critters.activeFact: Right now, pike may still be drawn to soft, shallow habitat and flooded edges that looked dead empty all winter.

critters.name: Spring Peeper
critters.summary: Tiny chorus frog now fully capable of turning one wet evening into a wall of sound.
critters.fact: Spring Peepers have a distinctive, high-pitched call that signals the start of spring in many North American forests.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, a warm damp night can light up marshes, ditches, and low spots with peeper calls that seem way too big for the body.

critters.name: American Bullfrog
critters.summary: Big pond frog still on the slower side of spring, waiting for steadier warmth before getting loud about it.
critters.fact: American Bullfrogs can produce loud, deep calls audible over half a mile away.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bullfrogs are often still quieter and less obvious than peepers and other early amphibian voices.

critters.name: Green Frog
critters.summary: Shoreline frog beginning to stir as wet edges and quiet shallows finally start feeling livable again.
critters.fact: Green frogs communicate using a distinctive "gunk" call to establish territory and attract mates.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, green frogs may start showing around thawed shorelines, calm coves, and soft wet pockets with a little sun on them.

critters.name: Leopard Frog
critters.summary: Early-moving spotted frog making the most of mild weather and shallow wetland warmups.
critters.fact: Leopard Frogs have distinctive dark spots and can leap over 20 times their body length.
critters.activeFact: Right now, leopard frogs may show up near sunny puddled ground, marsh edges, and wet grass before summer fully takes over.

critters.name: Garter Snake
critters.summary: One of spring’s first snakes, showing up anywhere the sun gets to work before lunch.
critters.fact: Garter snakes can swim and often hunt amphibians in water, making them excellent aquatic predators.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, a decent sunny afternoon can bring garter snakes out along trails, edges, gardens, and warm debris piles.

critters.name: Water Snake
critters.summary: Semi-aquatic snake easing toward regular activity as shorelines and shallows continue to warm.
critters.fact: Water snakes can breathe through their skin, allowing them to stay underwater longer than many other snakes.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sunny banks near calm open water may offer the best chance of seeing a water snake testing the season.

critters.name: Eastern Milk Snake
critters.summary: Secretive woodland snake starting to reappear around shelter that finally holds a little heat.
critters.fact: Eastern Milk Snakes mimic rattlesnakes by vibrating their tails to deter predators.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, milk snakes may turn up near barns, wood piles, brushy edges, and old cover on mild afternoons.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly
critters.summary: Northbound migrant still more promise than regular presence this far into northern Michigan spring.
critters.fact: Monarch butterflies migrate up to 3,000 miles to their wintering sites in Mexico and California.
critters.activeFact: Right now, monarchs are more a sign that the season is on its way than a butterfly you should expect to see every day.

critters.name: Swallowtail Butterfly
critters.summary: Big spring butterfly still waiting on steadier warmth and a stronger buffet of blooming plants.
critters.fact: Swallowtail butterflies use their tail-like wing extensions to confuse predators and avoid attacks.
critters.activeFact: At this point, swallowtails are still more likely to be sparse or absent than cruising confidently through local woods.

critters.name: Dragonfly
critters.summary: Future aerial hunter still mostly living the underwater chapter while spring gets its act together.
critters.fact: Dragonflies can fly in six directions: up, down, forward, backward, and side to side with great agility.
critters.activeFact: Right now, wetlands may look quiet above the surface while future dragonflies are still growing out of sight below it.

critters.name: Damselfly
critters.summary: Slender wetland insect still mostly hidden in nymph form this early in the northern season.
critters.fact: Damselflies fold their wings along their body when resting, unlike dragonflies that keep wings open.
critters.activeFact: Right now, damselflies are more likely tucked into pond margins below the surface than hovering delicately over them.

critters.name: Firefly
critters.summary: Summer lantern-maker still offstage while spring quietly builds the set around it.
critters.fact: Fireflies use bioluminescence to communicate and attract mates during warm summer nights.
critters.activeFact: In April, the woods may feel alive again, but firefly nights are still a good ways down the trail.

critters.name: Honeybee
critters.summary: Early pollinator taking every decent warm break spring is willing to hand out.
critters.fact: Honeybees communicate the location of food sources through a unique “waggle dance” inside the hive.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sunny windows can briefly put honeybees in the air even when mornings still start with a cold attitude.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Cold-tough queen bee out early and searching for both flowers and a place to start the empire.
critters.fact: Bumblebees can generate heat by vibrating their flight muscles, allowing them to fly in cooler temperatures.
critters.activeFact: In mid-April, a single large bumblebee drifting low across the ground is often a queen scouting flowers and future nest ground.

critters.name: Grasshopper
critters.summary: Still mostly absent from the spring lineup while wetter, earlier risers steal the show.
critters.fact: Grasshoppers communicate by rubbing their legs against their wings to create distinctive sounds called stridulation.
critters.activeFact: At this point in the year, spring is still setting the table long before grasshoppers start hopping through the scene.
